items.text: Dirty money isn't spendable until it's been washed, and the rinse cycle runs through financial institutions. BSA/AML drafts those institutions into the watch: they have to notice when a transaction is really an attempt to make illegitimate money look earned, and report it to the Treasury's FinCEN. Two reports carry most of the load — one fires on size alone, the other on judgment.

The first report is mechanical. Any cash transaction of $10,000 or more triggers a Currency Transaction Report (CTR), no suspicion required — the figure that matters is $10,000. Try to dodge it by slicing one deposit into smaller pieces, each kept under $10,000, and you've committed structuring — a crime in its own right.

The second report runs on judgment. Anything that looks like laundering gets a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) filed with FinCEN within 30 days of detecting the activity, so the standard deadline is 30 days. When no suspect can be named, the clock stretches by another 30 days, but the SAR can land no later than 60 days out. The dollar triggers depend on the suspect: a SAR is owed at $5,000 or more when a suspect can be identified, and at $25,000 or more regardless of whether any suspect is identified.

Filing a SAR comes with a gag: tipping off the subject, or even disclosing that a SAR exists, is illegal. The records of that filing — SARs along with CIP and account records — have to be kept on hand for at least 5 years from the filing date.

Separately, OFAC administers U.S. sanctions and maintains the SDN list of blocked parties. Institutions holding blocked property file the Annual Report of Blocked Property each year by September 30, so the cutoff date is September 30.
